disease.name: Cholangiocarcinoma
disease.note: Malignancy of bile duct epithelium. Classified as intrahepatic (peripheral), perihilar (Klatskin tumor — at bifurcation of hepatic ducts), or distal (extrahepatic). Perihilar tumors are most common (50-60%). Presents with painless progressive jaundice, pruritus, weight loss. Associated with PSC, liver flukes, and biliary malformations. Poor prognosis: 5-year survival 10-30% even with resection.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Resectable -> URGENT surgical referral; Unresectable/metastatic -> PALLIATIVE care/biliary drainage; Cholangitis -> EMERGENCY

presentingComplaints: Painless progressive yellowing of eyes and skin
Intense itching all over the body
Dark urine and pale clay-colored stools

historyFeatures.note: Painless, progressive jaundice is the classic presentation
Often severe and precedes jaundice; from bile salt deposition
Dull ache; not colicky like gallstones
Congenital biliary malformation; risk persists after cyst excision

examFeatures.note: Non-tender palpable gallbladder in distal CCA

subtypes.name: Intrahepatic Cholangiocarcinoma
subtypes.management: Surgical resection (lobectomy) if solitary and adequate future liver remnant. Adjuvant capecitabine or gemcitabine-based chemo. TACE or Y-90 for unresectable.

subtypes.name: Perihilar Cholangiocarcinoma (Klatskin Tumor)
subtypes.management: Bismuth I-II: resection with hilar lymphadenectomy + Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy. Bismuth III-IV: extended hepatectomy + caudate resection + biliary reconstruction. Portal vein embolization for small FLR. Liver transplant with neoadjuvant chemoradiation in selected patients.

subtypes.name: Distal (Extrahepatic) Cholangiocarcinoma
subtypes.management: Pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). Adjuvant capecitabine or gemcitabine-based chemo. Biliary stenting for palliation.

laboratory.name: Total and direct bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated conjugated (direct) bilirubin; biliary obstruction pattern

laboratory.name: ALP and GGT
laboratory.indication: markedly elevated in cholestasis; hallmark of CCA

laboratory.name: ALT and AST
laboratory.indication: mild to moderate elevation (if markedly elevated suggests hepatocellular injury or acute obstruction)

laboratory.name: CA 19-9
laboratory.indication: elevated in 60-70% of CCA; >100 U/mL suggests malignancy; can be elevated in cholangitis; CEA and CA125 also elevated

laboratory.name: CEA
laboratory.indication: may be elevated but less specific than CA19-9

laboratory.name: INR/PT
laboratory.indication: synthetic function; may be prolonged due to vitamin K malabsorption

laboratory.name: FBC
laboratory.indication: may show anemia of chronic disease

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: first-line: dilated bile ducts, level of obstruction, liver mass, portal vein patency; CBD >6mm dilated; intrahepatic duct dilation

imaging.name: CT abdomen triphasic (pancreatic protocol)
imaging.indication: tumor staging: size, location, vascular involvement (portal vein, hepatic artery), lymph nodes, metastasis; Bismuth classification for perihilar tumors

imaging.name: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography)
imaging.indication: non-invasive biliary mapping: stricture location, length, multifocality; 'shouldered' or irregular stricture suggests malignancy; double duct sign

imaging.name: MRI abdomen with contrast and diffusion-weighted imaging
imaging.indication: better soft tissue characterization than CT; liver parenchyma assessment; vascular involvement; peritoneal disease

imaging.name: ERCP with brushings + FISH for cytology
imaging.indication: therapeutic biliary drainage plus diagnostic sampling; brush cytology sensitivity ~40-60%; FISH polysomy increases sensitivity; can place stent for drainage

imaging.name: EUS with FNA of mass or lymph node
imaging.indication: tissue diagnosis with cytology; node staging; high sensitivity for distal CCA; lower for perihilar (intervening vessels)

imaging.name: PET-CT
imaging.indication: detect occult metastatic disease; SUV max correlates with aggressiveness; false positive in inflammation/cholangitis

imaging.name: Cholangioscopy (SpyGlass) with targeted biopsy
imaging.indication: direct visualization of bile duct; targeted biopsies of suspicious lesions; distinguish malignant from benign stricture

imaging.name: Diagnostic laparoscopy
imaging.indication: detect peritoneal or superficial liver metastases missed on cross-sectional imaging; performed before laparotomy for resection

management.protocolReference: NCCN Guidelines: Hepatobiliary Cancers; AASLD Practice Guidelines; ESMO Guidelines: Biliary Tract Cancer

pathways.criteria: Bismuth I-II perihilar or distal CCA; no vascular invasion; adequate FLR; no distant mets; good PS
pathways.treatment: Surgical resection: perihilar — hilar en bloc resection + Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy + caudate lobectomy ± extended hepatectomy. Distal — pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple). Portal vein embolization if FLR <30%. Preoperative biliary drainage if bilirubin >15 mg/dL or cholangitis. Adjuvant capecitabine x 6 months (BILCAP trial). Consider adjuvant S-1 in Asia.

pathways.criteria: Bismuth III-IV perihilar; bilateral vascular involvement; bilateral ductal extension; node-positive beyond porta hepatis
pathways.treatment: Biliary drainage (ERCP + metal stent, or PTC) — essential for symptom control and to allow chemotherapy. First-line: gemcitabine + cisplatin (ABC-02 trial) x 6-8 cycles. Second-line: FOLFOX (ABC-06 trial). Y-90 radioembolization for intrahepatic CCA. External beam radiation (SBRT) for selected patients. Clinical trials for targeted therapy (FGFR2 fusions, IDH1 mutations, HER2 amplification).

pathways.criteria: Distant metastases (liver, peritoneum, lung, distant nodes)
pathways.treatment: First-line systemic therapy: gemcitabine + cisplatin (ABC-02). Subsequent: FOLFOX (ABC-06), or clinical trial (FGFR inhibitor for FGFR2 fusions, ivosidenib for IDH1). Biliary drainage for symptom palliation. Pain management, nutritional support. Palliative care referral. Prognosis: median survival 6-12 months with chemo.

pathways.criteria: Unresectable perihilar CCA (Bismuth III-IV), lymph node negative, tumor <3cm, no metastasis — Mayo protocol
pathways.treatment: Neoadjuvant chemoradiation (external beam + 5-FU or capecitabine) + biliary stenting + exploratory laparotomy to rule out nodal/peritoneal disease. Then liver transplant. 5-year survival 53-65% in selected patients. Only available in specialized centers.

pathways.criteria: Jaundice from obstruction not amenable to curative resection
pathways.treatment: Endoscopic metal stent (self-expanding metal stent — SEMS) for distal obstruction. PTC + internal-external drain for hilar obstruction. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) may prolong stent patency and survival in selected perihilar CCA. Pruritus management: cholestyramine, rifampicin, antihistamines. Vitamin K for coagulopathy.
